coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical (bio)sensors based on carbon quantum dots, ionic liquid and gold nanoparticles for bisphenol A
coredata.dc:creator: Örenli, Demet
Kaçar Selvi, Ceren
Öztürk, Funda
Erden, Pınar Esra
Kılıç, Esma
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical (bio)sensors were developed for bisphenol A (BPA) determination. Screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was modified with ionic liquid 1- butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (IL), carbon quantum dots (CQD) and gold nanoparticles (AuNP) for the fabrication of the BPA sensor. Electrode surface composition was optimized for the deposition time of AuNP, amount of CQD and percentage of IL using the central composite design (CCD) method. The results of the CCD study indicated that maximum amperometric response was recorded when 9.8 μg CQD, 3% IL and 284 s AuNP deposition time were used in modification. Tyrosinase (Ty) was further modified on the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE to fabricate the biosensor. Analytical performance characteristics of the BPA sensor were investigated by differential pulse anodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry and the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor exhibited a linear response to BPA in the range of 2.0 × 10−8 – 3.6 × 10−6 M with a detection limit of 1.1 × 10−8 M. Amperometric measurements showed that the linear dynamic range and detection limit of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE were 2.0 × 10−8 – 4.0 × 10−6 M and 6.2 × 10−9 M, respectively. Analytical performance characteristics such as sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity were investigated for the presented (bio)sensors. The analytical applicability of the (bio)sensors to the analysis of BPA in mineral water samples was also tested.

(D) Effect of enzyme loading on amperometric BPA response of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (pH 6.5, 0.05 M PBS containing 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA).
(A) SEM images of (a) bare SPCE, (b) CQD/SPCE, (c) CQD−IL/SPCE, (d) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE and (e) Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE surfaces. (B) EDX spectra of (a) CQD−IL/SPCE and (b) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms (scan rate: 100 mVs−1) and (B) Nyquist plots (a) bare SPCE, (b) CQD/SPCE, (c) CQD−IL/SPCE, (d) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE, (e) Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA at different scan rates (v: a: 10, b: 20, c: 30, d: 40, e: 50, f: 60, g: 70, h: 80, i: 90 and j:100 mVs−1). (B) Plot of log I pa vs. log v at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. (C) Cyclic voltammograms of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE in absence (a) and in presence (b) of 2.0 × 10−4 M BPA at 50 mVs−1 scan rate (0.05 M PBS of pH 6.5).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE at different pH values in PBS solution. Plots of (B) I pa vs. pH and (C) E pa vs. pH at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (in 0.05 M PBS containing 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA). (D) Effect of pH on the amperometric response of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (in 0.05 M PBS containing 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA).

Effects of (A) accumulation potential and (B) accumulation time on the peak current of 2.0 × 10−6 M BPA on AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. (C) Effect of applied potential on the amperometric response of 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA on Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (pH 6.5 0.05 M PBS).

(A) Differential pulse voltammograms of BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE containing different concentrations of BPA ranging from 2.0 × 10−8 M to 3.6 × 10−6 M (B) corresponding calibration curve (0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5). (C) Current-time plots of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor to successive additions of BPA and (D) amperometric response current (μA) vs BPA concentration (0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5) at −0.20 V).

head.ce:title: Electrochemical (bio)sensors based on carbon quantum dots, ionic liquid and gold nanoparticles for bisphenol A

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical (bio)sensors were developed for bisphenol A (BPA) determination. Screen printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was modified with ionic liquid 1- butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (IL), carbon quantum dots (CQD) and gold nanoparticles (AuNP) for the fabrication of the BPA sensor. Electrode surface composition was optimized for the deposition time of AuNP, amount of CQD and percentage of IL using the central composite design (CCD) method. The results of the CCD study indicated that maximum amperometric response was recorded when 9.8 μg CQD, 3% IL and 284 s AuNP deposition time were used in modification. Tyrosinase (Ty) was further modified on the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE to fabricate the biosensor. Analytical performance characteristics of the BPA sensor were investigated by differential pulse anodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry and the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor exhibited a linear response to BPA in the range of 2.0 × 10−8 – 3.6 × 10−6 M with a detection limit of 1.1 × 10−8 M. Amperometric measurements showed that the linear dynamic range and detection limit of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE were 2.0 × 10−8 – 4.0 × 10−6 M and 6.2 × 10−9 M, respectively. Analytical performance characteristics such as sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity were investigated for the presented (bio)sensors. The analytical applicability of the (bio)sensors to the analysis of BPA in mineral water samples was also tested.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA), an organic monomer, is extensively used for the production of polycarbonate and epoxy resins [1,2]. These chemicals are used to manufacture daily use products and technological equipment such as food cans, beverage containers, baby bottles, food wrappers, compact disks, medical and dental devices, electronic apparatus and appliances and so on. In addition, thermal papers, dental fillings and sealants, flame-retardants and adhesives contain BPA [3–5]. Upon heating, BPA can be diffused into food and beverages from plastic bottles or food packaging containers [4]. Hence, humans may ingest low levels of BPA and its impact on human health has become a growing concern. Moreover, BPA can be released into the environment from various sources such as manufacturing and processing plants or the degradation of waste plastics [6]. A large number of reports have demonstrated that BPA is an important endocrine-disrupting chemical that can mimic and interfere with endogenous estrogen [7,8]. BPA exposure is associated with major health concerns, including diabetes, obesity, birth defects, polycystic ovarian syndrome, heart disease, hyperactivity, thyroid disorders, cancer development and immune system problems [5,9,10]. Simultaneously, BPA is an effective pollutant and contaminates both biological and abiotic environments [11]. Considering the negative effects of BPA on human health and the environment, the development of rapid and accurate methods for BPA determination is of great importance in environmental monitoring, food safety and health care.

BPA is detected by various analytical methods such as molecular fluorescence spectrometry [12], high-performance liquid chromatography [13], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [14], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [15], chemiluminescence [16], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [17] and capillary electrophoresis [18]. Most of these methods have serious disadvantages including high cost, long sample preparation time, unsuitability for on-site testing and the need for expensive equipment and well-trained analyst. Comparatively, electrochemical techniques are rapid, sensitive, cost-effective, simple and highly selective [19]. However, electrochemical sensors based on direct oxidation of BPA on conventional electrodes have found limited application in BPA determination due to poor response, surface fouling of the bare electrode and low selectivity [20,21]. The use of modified electrodes in electrochemical BPA determination is a promising approach to overcome these drawbacks [22]. Up to date many electrochemical sensors and biosensors based on modified electrodes have been reported for the determination of BPA [19]. Tyrosinase (Ty) enzyme is widely used in the fabrication of BPA biosensors [2,3]. Ty could catalyze the oxidation of BPA to the corresponding o-quinone in the presence of dissolved oxygen. The generated o-quinone could be electrochemically reduced at low operating potentials. The reduction current of o-quinone is used to determine BPA [2,23].

Of the various electrode modification materials reported so far, carbon-based nanomaterials have been the focus of researchers due to their unique features such as high stability, good electrocatalytic activity, large specific surface area and good conductivity. Carbon nanofibers, graphene and derivatives, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes and nanodiamonds have recently found a wide range of applications in the development of electrochemical (bio)sensors [24,25]. Among various carbon nanomaterials, nowadays carbon quantum dots (CQD) have drawn attention as novel sensing materials on the basis of their low toxicity, good conductivity, unique fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility, high stability, chemical inertness, large surface area, high catalytic activity, low cost and easy synthesis [26–28]. CQD, the zero-dimensional materials with small size (<10 nm), were first discovered in 2004 [29] and they have had noticeable applications in various fields, such as drug delivery [30], photocatalysis [31], sensors [27] and bioimaging [32]. Although the use of CQD in electrochemical (bio)sensors is limited the aforementioned properties make them promising candidates for electrode modification.

Despite the wide variety of metal nanoparticles available, gold nanoparticles (AuNP) have been presented as excellent electrode modification materials due to their good stability, high conductivity, easy synthesis and functionalization, biocompatibility and high catalytic activity [33]. A literature survey reveals that the coupling of a carbon-based nanomaterial with AuNP can drastically enhance the sensitivity of the modified electrode, further improving the analytical performance parameters of the electrochemical (bio)sensor [34].

Ionic liquids (IL) can be described as compounds composed of positively and negatively charged ions that remain liquid at room temperature. IL are widely used to fabricate modified electrodes due to their unique characteristics such as thermal and chemical stability, high conductivity, very low vapor pressure, wide potential window and good solubility [35,36]. The combination of IL with nanomaterials can create superior composites that might open up novel approaches for the fabrication of electrochemical (bio)sensors [37].

The aim of this study is to fabricate an electrochemical sensor and biosensor for the sensitive and selective determination of BPA and to compare their analytical performance parameters. In order to take full benefits of the three different types of modification materials CQD, AuNP and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (IL), it is attractive to make AuNP/CQD−IL composite, so that the individual properties of each modification material could be combined for inducing novel properties. Thus, SPCEs were first modified with CQD−IL mixture and AuNP was electrodeposited on the CQD−IL modified SPCE to fabricate a novel BPA sensor. Ty enzyme was further modified on the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE for the fabrication of the BPA biosensor. The optimization of electrode surface composition in (bio)sensor fabrication is usually carried out with the conventional one-variable-at-a-time approach [38,39]. However, this method is time-consuming and ignores the combination influence among the variables studied [40]. Due to the above-mentioned disadvantages, multivariate statistic techniques have been applied to optimize surface composition [41]. Response surface methodology, a combination of statistical and mathematical methods, can be utilized in analytical optimization. Central composite design (CCD) is one of the most efficient and significant methods used in response surface methodology. CCD provides advantages over the conventional one-variable-at-a-time method such as a decrease in the number of experiments, rapid gathering of experimental data, easier evaluation of the acquired data, and study of variable interactions [42,43]. In sensor and biosensor studies, CCD is mostly used for the optimization of experimental conditions such as pH, operating potential and temperature. Asturias-Arribas et al. [44] developed a chronoamperometric method for the determination of cocaine and applied CCD to optimize pH and operating potential. Alonso-Lomillo et al. [45] reported an electrochemical biosensor for levetiracetam determination and optimized H2O2 concentration, operating potential and pH of the buffer solution using CCD. CCD is also used for the optimization of electrode surface composition. Koçoğlu et al. [41] applied a five level three factorial CCD to determine the electrode composition for three variables: amounts of carboxylated multiwall carbon nanotubes, titanium dioxide nanoparticles, and glucose oxidase and used this electrode to fabricate an amperometric glucose biosensor. Ivanov et al. [46] developed an amperometric biosensor for the detection of acetylthiocholine and used a five level three factorial CCD to determine the optimum conditions for the enzyme immobilization with three variables: multiwalled carbon nanotubes, Concanavalin A and enzyme concentration. In our study, optimization of electrode surface composition was performed by CCD as an alternative to the one-variable-at-a-time approach. After the optimization of all experimental variables, the performance parameters of the (bio)sensors were determined and compared. Differential pulse anodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry (DPAAdSV) and amperometry techniques were used with the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor, respectively. The potential application of AuNP/CQD−IL and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL modified SPCEs in the determination of BPA was also addressed.

Ty (≥1000 unit/mg solid, from mushroom), carbon quantum dots (≥0.2% in water), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate, glutaraldehyde, (25% v/v in water), gold(III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), bisphenol A, Nafion (5 wt % in lower aliphatic alcohol and water mixture), potassium nitrate, sodium chloride, potassium ferricyanide, disodium monohydrogen phosphate, potassium ferrocyanide, calcium chloride, magnesium nitrate hexahydrate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate and potassium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol and methanol were obtained from Merck. Nafion was diluted with ethanol and water to obtain a 0.5% w/v concentration. BPA stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared in ethanol/water (50:50 v/v). 0.05 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was used in the experiments by adjusting the pH with NaOH or HCl.

Differential pulse anodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry (DPAAdSV) measurements were recorded on a Microstat 400 bipotentiostat/galvanostat (Dropsens, Spain). A μStat 8000 multi potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm Dropsens, Spain) was employed for the amperometry, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments. The morphology of the modified SPCEs was explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a QUANTA 400F field emission scanning electron microscope. A JXA-8230 Electron Probe Microanalyzer was utilized for EDX measurements. pH was adjusted with an ORION Model 720A pH/ion meter (Thermo Scientific, USA). The ionic liquid was dispersed in CQD using a vortex mixer from Isolab (Germany). All experiments were performed at room temperature.

SPCE (Dropsens C110) including a carbon working electrode, a silver pseudo-reference electrode and a carbon counter electrode was utilized for the development of the (bio)sensors. Amperometry experiments were carried out in stirred PBS (0.05 M, pH 6.5) at −0.20 V. EIS and CV experiments were conducted in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The EIS spectra were obtained in the frequency range of 0.1–10000 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. CV studies in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution were carried out in the range of −0.30 to +0.60 V at 100 mVs−1 scan rate. Differential pulse anodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry (DPAAdSV) measurements were conducted in 0.05 M PBS solution (pH 6.5). In a typical measurement, DPV conditions were as follows: scan range, from +0.2 to +0.4 V; pulse size, 10 mV; step size, 5 mV and pulse time, 0.001 s. Standart deviation of three measurements was used to obtain the error bars in the figures.

After conducting the 20 experiments, a second-order polynomial model for predicting the optimal value was expressed by the following equation:
(1)
Y = β 0 + β 1 [CQD] + β 2 [IL%] + β 3 [AuNP] + β 11 [CQD]2 + β 22 [IL%]2 + β 33 [AuNP]2 + β 12 [CQD] [IL%] +β 13 [CQD] [AuNP] + β 23 [IL%] [AuNP]

In the above equation, Y represents the predicted response; β 0 represents the model constant; β 1, β 2, β 3, β 11, β 22, β 33, β 12, β 13, and β 23 represent the coefficients for the linear, quadratic and interactive effects of [CQD], [IL%] and [AuNP] on the response, respectively. ANOVA with a confidence level of 95% (p = 0.05) was applied to explore the statistical importance of the variables and to test the validity of the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the AuNP/CQD−IL and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: 97 μL of CQD was mixed with 3 μL of ionic liquid and vortexed for 10 min. 5.0 μL of CQD−IL mixture was drop casted onto the SPCE working electrode surface and allowed to dry at room temperature to obtain the CQD−IL/SPCE. Then, CQD−IL/SPCE was further modified with AuNP. Electrodeposition of AuNP was carried out in 1.0 mM HAuCl4 and 0.20 M Na2SO4 solution at −0.40 V for 284 s. The modified electrode was denoted as AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE.

In order to fabricate the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor, 100 μL of Ty (50 U/μL) and 20 μL of 5% glutaraldehyde solution (GA) were mixed and 5 μL of Ty-GA solution was pipetted onto the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. The enzyme modified electrode was stored at 4 °C for 3 h to achieve the immobilization of Ty. Finally, 4 μL of Nafion solution (0.5%) was casted onto the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE and allowed to dry at 4 °C. Scheme 1 presents the fabrication steps of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor.

The 23 CCD was utilized to obtain the best surface composition for AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. The statistical importance of the variables was evaluated using ANOVA with a confidence level of 95%. Table 3 depicts the results of ANOVA for the experimental design presented in Table 2. The correlation coefficient R 2 was used to assess the reliability of the predicted model. In this study, R 2 was obtained as 0.8203, indicating a satisfactory correlation between experimental data and the predicted model. Fisher's F test was used to verify the significance of the model. The obtained F value (MS factor/MS error) of 5.07 at a low probability (p-value smaller than 0.05 (p = 0.0091)) confirms the significance of the model. The lack-of-fit p-value (0.1511) of the presented model was not significant (p > 0.05) and confirmed the acceptability of the model. Furthermore, A2 (p = 0.0002), B2 (p = 0.0219) and C2 (p = 0.0090) are significant model terms. After deleting the non-significant terms (p > 0.05), the following regression equation, representing the relationship between the response and the independent variables was obtained:
(2)
Y = 0.40–0.098 [CQD]2 − 0.047 [IL]2 − 0.056 [AuNP]2

Fig. 1 depicts 3D surface plots constructed according to Eq. (2) to determine the optimal value of variables for the electrode surface composition of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. The responses were plotted when two variables changed but the other variable is fixed at its central value. The 3D surface plots showed that the maximum amperometric response was recorded when 9.8 μg CQD, 3% IL and 284 s AuNP deposition time were used in modification. These optimum values were used for electrode modification in all experiments.

After the optimization of surface composition for AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE, Ty enzyme was immobilized onto the modified electrode to develop the BPA biosensor. Optimum enzyme loading was investigated using one-variable-at-a-time approach. Fig. 1D shows the amperometric response of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE prepared with five different units of Ty. The amperometric responses were recorded in 0.05 M PBS containing 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA. As can be seen from the figure, optimum enzyme loading was found to be 200 U. Increased loadings of the enzyme on the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE showed decreased responses. This result may be attributed to the oversaturation of the matrix which leads to a loss in activity [47].

SEM and EDX measurements were conducted to characterize the morphology of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE at different stages of modification. Fig. 2 A presents the SEM images of (a) bare SPCE, (b) CQD/SPCE, (c) CQD−IL/SPCE, (d) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE and (e) Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE surfaces. As can be seen from image A-b and image A-c, CQD and CQD−IL mixture create a smooth and homogeneous layer on the electrode surface. Image A-d represents well-deposited AuNP on the surface of CQD−IL/SPCE. The AuNP providing greater porosity can increase the effective surface area of the electrode. Moreover, the porous structure of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE offers a suitable surface for enzyme immobilization. After the immobilization of Ty onto AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE, the morphology changed significantly (image A-e) which indicates that the Ty enzyme was successfully immobilized onto the electrode surface. Fig. 2B–a shows the EDX spectra of CQD−IL/SPCE. The F peak confirms the presence of IL on electrode surface. The EDX spectra of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (Fig. 2B–b) indicate the presence of AuNP on the electrode surface. However, after the electrodeposition of AuNP on the electrode surface, the peak of F is not observed on the EDX spectra of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. This result can be attributed to the existence of an AuNP layer on the electrode surface which hinders the observation of F peak.

The modification steps of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE were monitored by CV and EIS measurements. Fig. 3 A depicts cyclic voltammograms of (a) bare SPCE, (b) CQD/SPCE, (c) CQD−IL/SPCE, (d) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE and (e) Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE recorded in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl between −0.30 V and +0.60 V at 100 mV s−1 scan rate. Well-defined oxidation and reduction peaks corresponding to the redox couple are observed in all voltammograms. After SPCE was modified with CQD (curve b), an increase in reduction and oxidation peak currents and a decrease of peak-to-peak separation (ΔE p) in comparison to the bare SPCE (curve a) was observed. This result can be ascribed to the larger effective surface area of the CQD/SPCE and accelerated electron transfer between the redox probe and the electrode [48]. When the CQD−IL mixture was modified on the SPCE (curve c), the peak currents increased more while ΔE p decreased, indicating that the CQD−IL composite can improve electron transmission between the redox probe and the electrode [49,50]. The peak currents significantly increased after AuNP was electrodeposited on CQD−IL/SPCE surface (curve d), which was mainly due to the higher effective surface area and better conductivity of AuNP modified electrode [51]. After the Ty was modified onto the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (curve e) the resulting electrode produced peak currents that were lower than the peak currents produced by CQD/SPCE, CQD−IL/SPCE and AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE indicating that the presence of nonconductive enzyme layer hindered the electron transfer [37].

EIS was conducted to investigate the impedance changes of the electrode during the modification steps. Impedance spectra can be presented in the form of Nyquist plots which reveals electron transfer kinetics and diffusion characteristics. In a typical Nyquist plot, the diameter of the semicircle corresponds to the charge transfer resistance (R ct) [52]. Fig. 3B depicts the Nyquist plots of (a) bare SPCE, (b) CQD/SPCE, (c) CQD−IL/SPCE, (d) AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE and (e) Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE recorded in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The CQD/SPCE (curve b) revealed a smaller semicircle than bare SPCE (curve a), indicating a lower electron transfer resistance. When the electrode surface was modified with CQD−IL the diameter of the semicircle decreased more (curve c) indicating that the CQD−IL composite can further improve the conductivity [50]. The charge transfer resistance significantly decreased after AuNP was electrodeposited on the CQD−IL/SPCE surface (curve d), which was ascribed to the highly conductive nature of AuNP [53]. The highest resistance was obtained with the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (curve e). This increase in charge transfer resistance can be attributed to the poor conductivity of enzymes [37].

The electrochemical behavior of BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was explored by CV in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5) in the presence of 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA. A well defined oxidation peak was obtained with AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE at a potential of about +0.31 V at 100 mVs−1 scan rate (Fig. 4 A). On the other hand, no cathodic peak was obtained at the reverse scan suggesting a totally irreversible electrode reaction [54]. In order to enlighten the nature of the electrochemical reaction that occurs at the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE, the effect of scan rate (v) on the oxidation peak current (I
pa ) and anodic peak potential (E pa) of BPA was evaluated using CV. Fig. 4A depicts the cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE with different scan rates (10−100 mVs−1). The relationship between the logarithm of peak current and the logarithm of scan rate (Fig. 4B) can be presented by the following equation: log I pa = 0.876 log v + 1.149 with R 2 = 0.9928. The slope value of the plot of log I pa versus log v is close to the theoretical value of 1.0, which indicates that the electrochemical oxidation of BPA at the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE is an adsorption-controlled electrode process [55]. Fig. 4A shows that the peak potentials shifted to more positive potential values as the scan rate increased from 10 to 100 mVs−1. This result shows that the electrooxidation step is not reversible [56].

The electrochemical behavior of BPA at Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was also investigated by CV in the absence and presence of 2.0 × 10−4 M BPA. Fig. 4C shows that the cyclic voltammograms of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE recorded in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5) exhibited two oxidation peaks and a reduction peak. In the presence of Ty enzyme, BPA is enzymatically oxidized first to corresponding o-biphenols, and then to o-quinones. Therefore, the first oxidation peak obtained at about +0.12 V can be ascribed to the oxidation of o-biphenols whereas the second oxidation peak at about +0.62 V corresponds to direct electrochemical oxidation of BPA. The cathodic peak obtained at about −0.27 V can be ascribed to the reduction of o-quinones. Similar redox processes were reported by Kochana et al. [57].

The effect of buffer pH on BPA determination at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was investigated by CV measurements in 0.05 M PBS containing 5.0 × 10−6 M BPA (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5B shows the relationship of I pa against pH. The oxidation peak currents of BPA increased as the pH increased from 5.5 to 6.5 and then decreased as the pH further increased from 6.5 to 8.5. The maximum peak current was achieved at pH 6.5 and this value was used in all sensor experiments. This result could be attributed to the following reason: The pKa value of BPA is 9.73 and the maximum voltammetric response to pH value is lower than the 9.73, indicating that non-dissociated BPA can be better adsorbed on the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE than the dissociated BPA [58].

The oxidation peak potential shifted towards less positive values with the increase in pH value, indicating the involvement of protons in the electrode reaction (Fig. 5C) [59]. The linear equation of E pa vs. pH is expressed as follows: E pa (V) = 0.0514 pH + 0.6129 with R2 = 0.9878. The value of the slope (0.0514) is close to the theoretical value (0.0592 V) indicating that equal numbers of protons and electrons are released in the electrooxidation of BPA [60]. On the basis of the experimental results and previous reports [6,61], the proposed mechanism for the electrochemical oxidation of BPA at the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE is as follows:
Image 1

The impact of buffer pH on BPA determination at Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was also investigated by amperometric measurements in 0.05 M PBS containing 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA. As shown in Fig. 5D response current of the biosensor increased as the pH increased from 6.0 to 6.5 and decreased afterwards. The highest amperometric response was recorded at pH 6.5 and this value was adopted as the optimum pH for the biosensor experiments. The optimum pH range for the free Ty was reported as 5.0–8.0 [62]. This result indicates that the AuNP/CQD−IL matrix and the immobilization procedure have no significant effect on the catalytic behavior of Ty.

Accumulation potential and time are two critical parameters in the adsorptive stripping voltammetry which could affect the peak current response of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. Therefore, in the DPAAdSV the effect of accumulation potential on the oxidation current of BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was studied in the potential range of (−0.20) V − (+0.20) V (Fig. 6 A) in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5) in the presence of 2.0 × 10−6 M BPA. The oxidation peak currents of BPA increased as the accumulation potential increased from −0.20 to 0.0 V and then decreased as the potential further increased from 0.0 V to +0.20. The maximum peak current was obtained at 0.0 V and this value was used in further studies. The influence of accumulation time on peak current was studied in the range of 15 s–180 s. The peak current increased as the accumulation time increased from 15 s to 120 s and decreased afterwards (Fig. 6B). Therefore, 120 s was selected as the optimum accumulation time.

The effect of applied potential on the amperometric response of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor to 5.7 × 10−6 M BPA was studied between (−0.40) – (−0.10)V (Fig. 6C). The highest amperometric response was obtained at −0.20 V and this potential was adopted as the optimum value for biosensor studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance characteristics of the BPA (bio)sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The DPAAdSV was used to explore the relationship between the anodic peak currents and concentration of BPA in order to test the feasibility of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE for the quantitative determination of BPA. Under optimum experimental conditions, different concentrations of BPA (2.0 × 10−8 to 3.6 × 10−6 M) were used to plot the calibration curves by DPAAdSV. Fig. 7 A shows the differential pulse voltammograms of BPA at AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE response to BPA was linear in the range of 2.0 × 10−8 − 3.6 × 10−6 M (Fig. 7B) which is wider than that of multiwalled carbon nanotube and gold nanoparticle-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (1.0 × 10−8 − 7.0 × 10−7 M) [63], poly(toluidine blue)/gold nanoparticle composite modified GCE (2.0 × 10−7 − 5.0 × 10−6 M) [64] and comparable to carboxylic multiwalled carbon nanotubes/poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride)/AuPd modified GCE (1.8 × 10−7−1.8 × 10− 5 M) [65] and gold nanoparticles-MoS2 nanoflowers/ionic liquid functionalized graphene modified GCE (5.0 × 10−8 − 4.0 × 10−6 M) [66]. The linear regression equation obtained with the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was: I pa(μA) = 89.88 × C BPA (mM) + 0.064 (R 2 = 0.9916). The detection limit (LOD) and quantification limit (LOQ) of the sensor were calculated by the following formula: LOD = 3 s/m and LOQ = 10 s/m, using the standard deviation of the intercept (s) and the slope of the calibration curve (m). The LOD and LOQ values for the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor were found to be 1.1 × 10−8 M and 3.5 × 10−8 M, respectively. The LOD of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor is lower than 6.0 × 10−8 M of carbon black/gold sononanoparticles modified sonogel-carbon electrode [67] and 6.0 × 10−8 M of carboxylic multiwalled carbon nanotubes/poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride)/AuPd modified GCE [65].

The response of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor to BPA was investigated by amperometry at −0.20 V in 0.05 M PBS (pH 6.5). Fig. 7C shows the current-time plots of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor to successive additions of BPA. The corresponding calibration curve exhibits a linear response to BPA ranging from 2.0 × 10−8 M to 4.0 × 10−6 M (Fig. 7D) which is similar to the dynamic range obtained with AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor. The linear range of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor is wider than the BPA biosensors based on multiwalled carbon nanotube, chitosan and Ty modified GCE (3.0 × 10−7 − 2.5 × 10−6 M) [68], nanographene, chitosan and Ty-modified GCE (1.0 × 10−7 − 2.0 × 10−6 M) [68] but comparable to poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene), poly(styrene sulfonate) and Ty modified SPE (2.0 × 10−8 − 9.0 × 10−6 M) [69] and cobalt-phtalocyanine and Ty-modified SPE (8.0 × 10−8 − 6.0 × 10−6 M) [69]. The sensitivity of Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor was found to be 589.4 μA/mM which was 6.5 fold higher than that of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor. The higher sensitivity achieved with the biosensor can be attributed to the presence of the Ty enzyme in the modification matrix. The LOD and LOQ values for the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor were calculated as 6.2 × 10−9 M and 2.1 × 10−8 M, respectively. The LOD of the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor was lower than that of TiO2/multi-walled carbon nanotubes/poly(diallyldimethylammoniumchloride)/nafion/Ty modified graphite electrode (6.6 × 10−8 M) [57] and tyrosinase, gold nanoparticles and thioctic acid amide self-assembled monolayers modified gold electrode (1.3 × 10−7 M) [70] but comparable to Pd nanoparticle loaded TiO2–silicon carbide modified GCE (4.3 × 10−9 M) [71]. The safe limit for the release of BPA at 22 nM (50 μgL−1) was recommended by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) [72]. Thus, the detection limits of the presented AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (11 nM) and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE (6.2 nM) can provide enough sensitivity for real applications.

In order to investigate the reproducibility of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor, five modified electrodes were prepared independently and used for BPA determination by plotting calibration curves. The relative standard deviation of the sensitivities was found to be 4.4% with the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor and 4.3% with the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor, respectively. This result shows the high reproducibility of the presented (bio)sensors.

The selectivity of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor and Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor was investigated in the presence of possible interferences namely Ca2+, Na+, Cl−, K+, PO4 3−, Mg2+, NO3 −, ethanol and methanol. DPAAdSV response of the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor to successive additions of 5.0 × 10−7 M BPA and 1.0 × 10−5 M of each interference into a stirred 0.05 M PBS solution were recorded. Ca2+, Na+, Cl−, K+, Mg2+, NO3 − and ethanol did not cause any considerable interference to the detection of 5.0 × 10−7 M BPA (<3.0%). PO4 3− and methanol caused only 4.7% and 3.4% interference on the response of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE, respectively. Therefore, the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE sensor can be considered to be very specific. On the other hand, amperometric measurements indicated that the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE biosensor also exhibited excellent selectivity for BPA detection. The interference caused by Ca2+, Na+, Cl−, K+, PO4 3−, Mg2+, NO3 −, ethanol and methanol to 5.0 × 10−7 M BPA was <2.5%.

The analytical performances of the proposed (bio)sensors were explored with the analysis of spiked mineral water samples. Recoveries were obtained by determination of BPA concentration in the mineral water samples after spiking with known concentrations of BPA (Table 4 ). The recoveries of three analyses performed with Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE were found to be 97.4, 102.6, and 99.1% for the spiked BPA concentration. The average recovery (99.7%) which is near 100% confirms the reliability of the method for BPA determination. On the other hand, an average recovery of 101.9% was obtained with the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. The results of the real sample analysis show that the proposed (bio)sensors were highly effective and accurate for the determination of BPA in real samples.

In this study, CQD, IL and AuNP were used as efficient electrode modification materials to fabricate electrochemical (bio)sensors for BPA determination. Optimization of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE surface composition was performed by 23 CCD. From the comparison of the analytical performance parameters of the presented (bio)sensors, it can be concluded that both of them can be efficiently used for the determination of BPA. Based on the AuNP/CQD−IL composites, the electrochemical (bio)sensors showed good analytical performance for BPA, in terms of low detection limit, good sensitivity, wide linear range, high selectivity and satisfactory reproducibility. The sensitivity achieved with the Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE was 6.5 fold higher than that of AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE which was ascribed to the incorporation of Ty into the matrix. Moreover, Ty/AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE exhibited a lower detection limit and better selectivity than the AuNP/CQD−IL/SPCE. The presented (bio)sensors were successfully used to determine BPA in spiked mineral water samples and recoveries around 100% were obtained. These results revealed the attractiveness of these disposable SPCE-based (bio)sensors thanks to the synergic combination of CQD, IL and AuNP. Although the use of CQD in electrochemical (bio)sensor development is new, the attractive properties of this nanomaterial, such as good conductivity, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high stability, chemical inertness, large surface area, high catalytic activity and low cost make CQD based composites promising materials for the construction of electrochemical (bio)sensors and they may find more applications in the future.
Demet Örenli: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation.
Ceren Kaçar Selvi: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Visualization.
Funda Öztürk: Conceptualization, Formal analysis.
Pınar Esra Erden: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
Esma Kılıç: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
